questions.text: Sum of the masses of constituent nucleons as compared to the mass of the resultant nucleus is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Incorrect as per fact or reaction.
Incorrect as per fact or reaction.

The sum of the masses of the constituent nucleons (protons and neutrons) is actually greater than the mass of the resultant nucleus. This difference in mass is known as the mass defect.The mass defect is given by the equation:Δm=Zmp​+(A−Z)mn​−mnuc​where:Z is the number of protons,mp​ is the mass of a proton,A is the total number of nucleons,mn​ is the mass of a neutron, andmnuc​ is the mass of the nucleus
Incorrect as per fact or reaction.

questions.explanation: The sum of the masses of the constituent nucleons (protons and neutrons) is actually greater than the mass of the resultant nucleus. This difference in mass is known as the mass defect.The mass defect is given by the equation:Δm=Zmp​+(A−Z)mn​−mnuc​where:Z is the number of protons,mp​ is the mass of a proton,A is the total number of nucleons,mn​ is the mass of a neutron, andmn​uc is the mass of the nucleus

questions.text: In the time constant of RC circuit, how much charge is stored, out of maximum charge q?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: In an RC circuit, the time constant (τ) is defined as the time it takes for the charge to reach approximately 63.2% of its maximum value. This is due to the nature of the exponential charging and discharging behavior of RC circuits.Mathematically, this is represented as:q(t)=Q(1−e−t/RC)where:q(t) is the charge at time t,Q is the maximum charge,R is the resistance,C is the capacitance,e is the base of the natural logarithm (approximately equal to 2.71828).At t = RC (one time constant), the equation simplifies to:q(RC)=Q(1−e−1) ≈0.632QSo, at one time constant, approximately 63.2% of the maximum charge Q is stored in the capacitor.
Incorrect as per formulas;q(t)=Q(1−e−t/RC)
Incorrect as per formulas;q(t)=Q(1−e−t/RC)
Incorrect as per formulas;q(t)=Q(1−e−t/RC)

questions.explanation: In an RC circuit, the time constant (τ) is defined as the time it takes for the charge to reach approximately 63.2% of its maximum value. This is due to the nature of the exponential charging and discharging behavior of RC circuits.Mathematically, this is represented as: q(t)=Q(1−e−t/RC)where:q(t) is the charge at time t,Q is the maximum charge,R is the resistance,C is the capacitance,e is the base of the natural logarithm (approximately equal to 2.71828).At t = RC (one time constant), the equation simplifies to:q(RC)=Q(1−e−1) ≈0.632QSo, at one time constant, approximately 63.2% of the maximum charge Q is stored in the capacitor.

questions.text: The electric field at a distance of 20cm from 4µC charge is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The electric field E due to a point charge q at a distance r from the charge is given by Coulomb’s law: E=kq​/r2where:k is Coulomb’s constant = 8.99×109Nm2/C2q is the charger is the distance from the charge.Substituting the given values (q = 4µC = 4x10−6C) & (r = 20cm = 0.2m) into the equation, we get: E=(8.99×109Nm2/C2)×(4×10−6C​)/(0.2m)2 =(8.99×109Nm2/C2)×(4×10−6C​)/(0.04m2 or 4×10-2m2) =8.99×109×10-6×102 N/C =8.99×105N/C ≈9x105 N / C
Incorrect as per given condition and formula: E=kq​/r2
Incorrect as per given condition and formula: E=kq​/r2
Incorrect as per given condition and formula: E=kq​/r2

questions.explanation: The electric field E due to a point charge q at a distance r from the charge is given by Coulomb’s law: E=kq​/r2where:k is Coulomb’s constant = 8.99×109Nm2/C2q is the charger is the distance from the charge.Substituting the given values (q = 4µC = 4x10−6C) & (r = 20cm = 0.2m) into the equation, we get: E=(8.99×109Nm2/C2)×(4×10−6C​)/(0.2m)2 =(8.99×109Nm2/C2)×(4×10−6C​)/(0.04m2 or 4×10-2m2) =8.99×109×10-6×102 N/C =8.99×105N/C ≈9x105 N / C

questions.text: Resistance of a wire is 'R' ohms. The wire is stretched to four times its length, then its resistance in ohms is
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Incorrect as per given condition and formula: R=ρL​/A
Incorrect as per given condition and formula: R=ρL​/A

The resistance of a wire is given by the formula: R=ρL​/Awhere:R is the resistance,ρ is the resistivity of the material,L is the length of the wire, andA is the cross-sectional area of the wire.When the wire is stretched to four times its original length, the volume (and therefore the mass) of the wire remains constant. So, if the length is quadrupled, the cross-sectional area must be quartered.Let’s denote the new resistance as R′, the new length as L′, and the new cross-sectional area as A′. We have: R′=ρL′​/A′Substituting L′=4L and A′=A/4​, we get: R′=ρ​(4L)/(A/4) ​=16ρL/A​ =16RSo, the resistance of the wire will be 16 times the original resistance
Incorrect as per given condition and formula: R=ρL​/A

questions.explanation: The resistance of a wire is given by the formula: R=ρL​/Awhere:R is the resistance,ρ is the resistivity of the material,L is the length of the wire, andA is the cross-sectional area of the wire.When the wire is stretched to four times its original length, the volume (and therefore the mass) of the wire remains constant. So, if the length is quadrupled, the cross-sectional area must be quartered.Let’s denote the new resistance as R′, the new length as L′, and the new cross-sectional area as A′. We have: R′=ρL′​/A′Substituting L′=4L and A′=A/4​, we get: R′=ρ​(4L)/(A/4) ​=16ρL/A​ =16RSo, the resistance of the wire will be 16 times the original resistance.

questions.text: A charge is at rest in a magnetic field, then the magnetic force on charge is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Incorrect as per given condition and formula; F=q(v×B)
﻿Incorrect as per given condition and formula; F=q(v×B)
Incorrect as per given condition and formula; F=q(v×B)a

The magnetic force on a charge is given by the Lorentz force law, which can be stated as: F=q(v×B)where:F is the magnetic force,q is the charge,v is the velocity of the charge, andB is the magnetic field.If a charge is at rest (i.e., its velocity v is zero), then the magnetic force on the charge is zero. This is because the cross product of zero (the velocity) and any vector (the magnetic field) is zero. So, even if the charge is in a magnetic field, if it’s not moving, it won’t experience a magnetic force.

questions.explanation: The magnetic force on a charge is given by the Lorentz force law, which can be stated as: F=q(v×B)where:F is the magnetic force,q is the charge,v is the velocity of the charge, andB is the magnetic field.If a charge is at rest (i.e., its velocity v is zero), then the magnetic force on the charge is zero. This is because the cross product of zero (the velocity) and any vector (the magnetic field) is zero. So, even if the charge is in a magnetic field, if it’s not moving, it won’t experience a magnetic force.

questions.text: A 2m wire carrying current 5A is at right angle to uniform magnetic field of 0.2 Web/m². The force on wire will be:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Incorrect as per condition and formula: F=BILsinθ
Incorrect as per condition and formula: F=BILsinθ

The force on a wire carrying a current in a magnetic field can be calculated using the formula: F=BILsinθwhere:F is the force,B is the magnetic field strength,I is the current,L is the length of the wire, andθ is the angle between the current and the magnetic field.In this case, the wire is at a right angle to the magnetic field, so θ = 90 degrees and sin(θ) is 1. Substituting the given values: F=(0.2Web/m2)⋅(5A)⋅(2m)⋅sin(90°) =2NSo, the force on the wire is 2 Newtons.
Incorrect as per condition and formula: F=BILsinθ

questions.explanation: The force on a wire carrying a current in a magnetic field can be calculated using the formula: F=BILsinθwhere:F is the force,B is the magnetic field strength,I is the current,L is the length of the wire, andθ is the angle between the current and the magnetic field.In this case, the wire is at a right angle to the magnetic field, so θ = 90 degrees and sin(θ) is 1. Substituting the given values: F=(0.2Web/m2)⋅(5A)⋅(2m)⋅sin(90°) =2NSo, the force on the wire is 2 Newtons.

questions.text: The Component in generator which consumes energy is called:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The component in a generator that consumes energy is called the load. In the context of electrical systems, a load is any device or component that consumes electrical power. This could include appliances like a refrigerator or a television, or components like resistors in a circuit. The energy produced by the generator is used to power these loads.
Split rings are component of a generator, but they do not consume energy in the same way a load does. They have specific role in the operation of a generator.
Commutator is component of a generator, but it does not consume energy in the same way a load does. It have specific role in the operation of a generator.
Capacitor is component of a generator, but it does not consume energy in the same way a load does. It have specific role in the operation of a generator.

questions.explanation: The component in a generator that consumes energy is called the load. In the context of electrical systems, a load is any device or component that consumes electrical power. This could include appliances like a refrigerator or a television, or components like resistors in a circuit. The energy produced by the generator is used to power these loads. Capacitors, commutators, and split rings are components of a generator, but they do not consume energy in the same way a load does. They have different roles in the operation of a generator. For example, a commutator helps to switch the direction of current in a generator.

questions.text: The circuit in which current and voltage are in phase, the power factor is:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: A power factor of 1 indicates that the voltage and current are in phase and have a low-harmonic content. A power factor of 0 indicates that the voltage and current are 90-degrees out of phase.

questions.text: An alternating voltage is given by 30sin157 the frequency of alternating voltage is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Incorrect as per formula and condition: Vt=Vm​sin(ωt) f=ω/2π​

The equation for the alternating voltage you’ve provided is in the form of Vt=Vm​sin(ωt)where:Vm​ is the peak voltage, andω is the angular frequency.In your case, Vm​=30 and ω=157.The frequency f of the alternating voltage can be found from the angular frequency ω using the relation f=ω/2π​Substituting ω=157 into the equation gives: f=157/2π​ ≈25HzSo, the frequency of the alternating voltage is approximately 25 Hz.
Incorrect as per formula and condition: Vt=Vm​sin(ωt) f=ω/2π​
Incorrect as per formula and condition: Vt=Vm​sin(ωt) f=ω/2π​

questions.explanation: The equation for the alternating voltage you’ve provided is in the form of Vt=Vm​sin(ωt)where:Vm​ is the peak voltage, andω is the angular frequency.In your case, Vm​=30 and ω=157.The frequency f of the alternating voltage can be found from the angular frequency ω using the relation f=ω/2π​Substituting ω=157 into the equation gives: f=157/2π​ ≈25HzSo, the frequency of the alternating voltage is approximately 25 Hz.

questions.text: If an atom exists in excited state n = 3 the number of transitions that take place is
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Incorrect as per fact and formula; n(n−1)/2​

An atom in an excited state can transition to any of the lower energy levels. If an atom is in the excited state where the principal quantum number n=3It can transition to states with n=2 or n=1Each transition corresponds to the emission of a photon with a specific energy (or wavelength). The number of possible transitions is given by the formula n(n−1)/2​where n is the initial energy level. So, for an atom in the n=3 state, the number of possible transitions is: = 3(3−1)​/2 =3These transitions aren=3→2 ,n=3→1 n=2→1
Incorrect as per fact and formula; n(n−1)/2​
Incorrect as per fact and formula; n(n−1)/2​

questions.explanation: An atom in an excited state can transition to any of the lower energy levels. If an atom is in the excited state where the principal quantum number n=3It can transition to states with n=2 or n=1Each transition corresponds to the emission of a photon with a specific energy (or wavelength). The number of possible transitions is given by the formula n(n−1)/2​where n is the initial energy level. So, for an atom in the n=3 state, the number of possible transitions is: = 3(3−1)​/2 =3These transitions aren=3→2 ,n=3→1 n=2→1
